attributes.stem:
A 34-year-old obese man presents with a 2 hour history of post-prandial upper abdominal pain. The patient has experienced previous similar episodes, but has not sought medical attention before. He is afebrile and his physical examination findings are normal other than some tenderness in the right upper quadrant. His haematological and biochemical studies are all normal.

The diagnosis of biliary colic is made and an elective cholecystectomy is undertaken. The X-rays shown (Images 1, 2) were performed during the operation. The cystic duct is cannulated with approximately 10 ml of contrast injected (Image 2).
attributes.stemQuestion: With regards to the cystic duct, which one of the following statements is most correct?
options: It joins the right hepatic duct
It sits immediately anterior to the first part of the duodenum
It joins the common hepatic duct
The spiral valves of Heister prevent back flow of bile into the gallbladder
It sits adjacent to segment VII of the liver
attributes.explanation:
This patient was diagnosed with biliary colic, typically caused by a gallstone causing transient cystic duct obstruction. Although the term 'colic' refers to pain that comes and goes, the pain in biliary colic is typically constant.

Whether a gallstone becomes obstructed in the cystic duct, common bile duct, or ampulla of vater will help determine the diagnosis of a patient with biliary pathology, as well as the treatment that is likely to be needed. Therefore, it is essential to understand the anatomy of the biliary tree. During an intra-operative cholangiogram depicted in this patient (Image), a clip is applied at the junction of the cystic duct and gallbladder, and a small ductotomy is made in the cystic duct to allow a cannula to be inserted and contrast injected into the biliary system. The clip to the cystic duct will stop contrast refluxing into the gallbladder and outline the right (arrow B) and left hepatic (arrow C) ducts, common hepatic duct (arrow D), common bile duct (arrow E), and the flow of contrast entering the duodenum.

The gallbladder sits inferior to segments V and IVb of the liver, while segment VII of the liver sits laterally to the right hepatic vein. The gallbladder empties bile into the cystic duct. The cystic duct contains spiral valves of Heister which although their functions are not fully known, it is hypothesised these spiral valves maintain patency of the cystic duct. Within the liver, the left and right hepatic ducts are present. These hepatic ducts join each other extra-hepatically, forming the common hepatic duct.

The cystic duct most commonly joins the common hepatic duct as demonstrated in this cholangiogram, however, very rarely, in 0.4% of the population it can join the right hepatic duct.

Once the cystic duct combines with the common hepatic duct, the common bile duct is formed. The common bile duct transcends anterolaterally within the hepatoduodenal ligament, combining with the main pancreatic duct at the ampulla of vater - see arrow (Image 2). From the ampulla of vater, the biliary tree finally drains into the second part of the duodenum, and therefore the cystic duct does not sit immediately anterior to the duodenum.
learningPoints: Biliary Colic typically presents with RUQ constant pain starting after a fatty meal, and should return to baseline within 8 hours.

The cystic duct joins the common hepatic duct to form the common bile duct, which travels anterolaterally to combine with the main pancreatic duct at the ampulla of Vater, finally emptying into the second part of the duodenum.
references: Sigmon DF, Dayal N, Meseeha M. Biliary Colic. [Updated 2021 Aug 6]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430772/
Tsujino, T., Samarasena, J. & Chang, K., 2018. EUS anatomy of the liver segments. Endoscopic ultrasound, 7(4), pp.246–251.
Abou-Khalil, Jad E., MD, MSc, FRCSC & Bertens, Kimberly A., MD, MSc, FRCSC, 2018. Embryology, Anatomy, and Imaging of the Biliary Tree. The Surgical clinics of North America, 99(2), pp.163–174.
Pina, L.N. et al., 2015. Surgical Considerations of the Cystic Duct and Heister Valves. Surgery journal (New York), 1(1), pp.e23–e27.
